item_name: Charging infrastructure and spatial EV adoption models
model_family: A mixed literature family combining cross-country and regional adoption regressions, two-sided market and indirect network-effect models, spatial econometric models, metro-level accessibility regressions, behavioral mediation models, and land-use/accessibility clustering studies.
scope_definition: This literature examines how EV adoption responds to charging station availability, perceived range or charging anxiety, spatial accessibility, land-use patterns, and regional heterogeneity. It covers direct adoption outcomes such as EV market share, registrations, purchases, or adoption intent, and also includes nearby accessibility and land-use studies when they are used to explain why infrastructure effects differ across places.
representative_papers: Sierzchula, Bakker, Maat, and van Wee (2014), Energy Policy, 'The influence of financial incentives and other socio-economic factors on electric vehicle adoption' (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enpol.2014.01.043). Cross-country regression for 30 countries; finds charging infrastructure is one of the strongest correlates of EV market share.

Li, Tong, Xing, and Zhou (2017), Journal of the Association of Environmental and Resource Economists, 'The Market for Electric Vehicles: Indirect Network Effects and Policy Design' (https://doi.org/10.1086/689702). Estimates two-sided feedback between charger deployment and EV sales across 353 U.S. metro areas and shows infrastructure subsidies can be highly effective because of network effects.

Narassimhan and Johnson (2018), Environmental Research Letters, 'The role of demand-side incentives and charging infrastructure on plug-in electric vehicle adoption: analysis of US States' (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aad0f8). U.S. state panel linking charger density and purchase incentives to BEV and PHEV adoption, with stronger effects for rebates and important heterogeneity by vehicle type and range.

White, Carrel, Shi, and Sintov (2022), Energy Research & Social Science, 'Why are charging stations associated with electric vehicle adoption? Untangling effects in three United States metropolitan areas' (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.erss.2022.102663). Survey-based metropolitan study showing charger density affects adoption intent mainly through subjective norms and only partly through range anxiety.

He, Zhou, Chen, Wang, Shen, Wang, and Li (2021), Transportation Letters, 'Examining the spatial mode in the early market for electric vehicles adoption: evidence from 41 cities in China' (https://doi.org/10.1080/19427867.2021.1917217). Spatial econometric model showing charging piles, income, density, and education shape early EV adoption with some spillovers.

Qian, Zhang, Chen, and Xiao (2024), Transportation Research Part D, 'Spatial cross-side network effect of charging stations on electric vehicle adoption' (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trd.2024.104400). Spatial panel for Tesla in the U.S.; finds adoption rises with both within-state and neighboring-state charging accessibility, with fast-charging corridors especially important.

Carlton and Sultana (2022), Journal of Urban Mobility, 'Electric vehicle charging station accessibility and land use clustering: A case study of the Chicago region' (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.urbmob.2022.100019). Shows EV charging accessibility is uneven across urban and suburban land-use regimes, clarifying how land use structures spatial opportunities for EV use.

Choi, Yang, Omidi, and Anwar (2026), Journal of Transport Geography, 'Spatial accessibility of electric vehicle charging infrastructure and adoption rate in the U.S. metropolitan areas' (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtrangeo.2026.104638). Metro-level study showing the distribution and configuration of chargers matter in addition to simple charger counts.

Lou, Shen, Niemeier, and Hultman (2024), Nature Communications, 'Income and racial disparity in household publicly available electric vehicle infrastructure accessibility' (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-49481-w). Demonstrates that accessibility gaps vary strongly across income, race, dwelling type, and urban-rural context, which is important for heterogeneous adoption potential.
search_queries_used: electric vehicle adoption charging infrastructure spatial model range anxiety accessibility land use literature

Examining the spatial mode in the early market for electric vehicles adoption evidence from 41 cities in China
unit_of_analysis: Mixed across studies: countries, U.S. metropolitan areas, U.S. states, Chinese cities, households or survey respondents, and neighborhood or tract accessibility surfaces.
adoption_mechanism: Charging infrastructure affects EV adoption through several linked channels: reducing expected charging inconvenience and range anxiety, expanding feasible trip space, increasing visibility and legitimacy of EV use, and creating indirect network effects in which more chargers raise EV demand and a larger EV fleet justifies further charger investment. Spatial models add spillovers, meaning infrastructure in neighboring regions or along highway corridors can influence local adoption by supporting long-distance travel.
heterogeneity_representation: Heterogeneity is represented by charger type (fast versus destination or slow charging), vehicle type (BEV versus PHEV), geography (urban versus rural, core versus periphery, neighboring regions, highway centrality), socioeconomics (income, education, population density), built environment and land use, and equity dimensions such as race, dwelling type, and access to private charging.
social_influence_representation: Usually weak or absent in econometric adoption papers, but explicit in White et al. (2022), which models subjective norms as a mediator and finds that charger density often works by signaling that EVs are normal and socially supported. In most other studies, social influence is implicit and absorbed into spatial clustering, diffusion, or fixed effects rather than directly measured.
spatial_or_infrastructure_representation: The literature moves from simple charger counts or charger-per-capita ratios toward richer spatial measures: two-sided infrastructure-demand feedback, spatial lags of neighboring infrastructure, accessibility indices based on distance and regional configuration, highway-network centrality, and land-use cluster measures that distinguish whether charging is embedded in residential, commercial, or corridor environments.
price_or_cost_representation: Most studies include vehicle purchase incentives, tax credits, rebates, or vehicle-cost proxies, and some control for income or market conditions. Detailed charging prices, waiting time, reliability, or generalized access costs are usually missing, so infrastructure is often represented as quantity or accessibility rather than total user cost.
policy_levers: Purchase rebates and tax credits for BEVs and PHEVs
Destination charging at commercial and hospitality land uses
Targeted infrastructure build-out for underserved urban, suburban, and rural areas
Local government demonstration programs and visibility campaigns
Land-use and zoning support for charging in multifamily and activity-center locations
Industrial policy and local manufacturing support that reinforce EV ecosystem formation
data_sources: Vehicle sales, registrations, or market-share data at country, metro, state, or city level
Public charger inventories from sources such as the U.S. Department of Energy Alternative Fuels Data Center, Tesla network data, or local infrastructure inventories
Socioeconomic and demographic data from census and statistical agencies
GIS, network, and land-use datasets describing urban form, distances, and activity clusters
Consumer survey data on EV intent, range anxiety, perceived mobility restrictions, and subjective norms
Administrative or policy datasets on subsidies, rebates, tax incentives, and demonstration programs
calibration_or_estimation: Common approaches include multiple linear regression, ordinary least squares, panel regressions with controls and fixed effects, simultaneous-equation or two-sided market estimation, mediation analysis for behavioral mechanisms, and spatial econometric models such as spatial lag or Spatial Durbin-type specifications. Accessibility studies often construct GIS or network-based indicators and then regress adoption rates on those indicators. Fully structural dynamic calibration of joint charger-and-vehicle diffusion remains rare.
validation_approach: Validation is mostly internal: robustness checks across alternative infrastructure measures, charger-type splits, BEV versus PHEV specifications, different spatial weights or neighbor definitions, and stress tests on regression models. Predictive validation, external validation across regions, and micro-level validation of whether accessibility metrics match realized charging behavior are limited.
main_findings: The literature consistently finds that charging infrastructure is positively associated with EV adoption, but the strength of the effect depends on how infrastructure is measured and where it is located. Raw charger counts matter, yet spatial accessibility and network configuration matter more when the research measures them directly. Fast charging and corridor connectivity can affect adoption beyond the local region because they reduce long-distance travel concerns. Range anxiety is only part of the mechanism; social visibility and perceived normality of EV ownership also matter. Infrastructure effects are strongly heterogeneous across income, density, land use, and urban-rural context, which means uniform rollout strategies can miss the places where additional accessibility would have the largest marginal adoption effect.
limitations: Much of the evidence is observational and vulnerable to endogeneity because chargers are often deployed where EV demand is already rising. Many studies use coarse spatial units such as states or metropolitan areas, which obscures within-region inequities and home-versus-public charging differences. Behavioral mechanisms are measured directly in only a small subset of papers. Land-use and accessibility studies often explain charging opportunity rather than observed vehicle purchase, while adoption studies often simplify infrastructure to counts or density and omit reliability, queueing, pricing, and off-street parking constraints.
relevance_to_affordance_model: This literature is highly relevant because it treats charging infrastructure as an affordance: not just a stock of chargers, but a geographically situated opportunity to complete trips, reduce uncertainty, and make EV ownership workable for specific households in specific places. For an affordance-oriented model, the strongest lesson is that accessibility, configuration, and context matter more than raw infrastructure totals, and that the same charger can create very different adoption opportunities depending on land use, trip patterns, dwelling type, and nearby network coverage.
transferable_mechanisms: Two-sided network effects between EV fleet growth and charger deployment
Spatial spillovers from neighboring regions and corridor infrastructure
Differentiation between fast-charging and destination-charging effects
Accessibility-based infrastructure metrics instead of simple charger counts
Behavioral mediation through subjective norms and reduced range anxiety
Land-use clustering as a mechanism for unequal charging opportunity
Regional interaction effects with income, density, education, housing, and urban form
implementation_guidance: Model home, workplace, destination, and corridor charging separately instead of using a single infrastructure stock. Build accessibility indicators that reflect distance, spatial configuration, and cross-border travel opportunities, not just charger counts. Include spatial lag terms or neighbor exposure so that nearby infrastructure can affect local adoption. Interact infrastructure variables with dwelling type, off-street parking access, income, density, and land use to capture heterogeneous affordances. Where possible, address endogeneity with lagged deployment, quasi-experimental variation, or joint vehicle-infrastructure modeling. For policy simulation, prioritize charger placement in locations that close accessibility gaps rather than simply maximizing the number of installed plugs.
key_gaps_for_research: Major gaps include weak causal identification of infrastructure effects, limited joint modeling of home charging and public charging, poor treatment of charger reliability and waiting time, and insufficient integration of land use with household-level purchase behavior. The literature also needs more dynamic models linking where chargers are built, who can actually access them, and how adoption evolves over time across neighborhoods. Equity-sensitive modeling remains underdeveloped, especially for renters, multifamily housing, rural regions, and places where off-street parking is scarce.
